content:
# Leviticus Chapter 6 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic) | **Hebrew:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 60r–60v | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 90–91 (pa_0090–pa_0091)

---

*[Note on versification: MT Lev 6:1-23 = English Lev 6:8-30. MT chapter 6 begins at what English Bibles call 5:20; English 6:1-7 = MT 5:20-26, already translated in LEV_005. English verse numbers are used throughout; MT equivalents are noted at section headings.]*

---

**8** [MT 6:1] And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**9** [MT 6:2] "Command Aaron and his sons, saying: This is the instruction of the burnt offering — the burnt offering shall remain on the hearth on the altar all night until morning, and the fire of the altar shall be kept burning on it.

**10** [MT 6:3] The priest shall put on his linen garment and put his linen undergarment on his body, and he shall take up the ashes that the fire has consumed of the burnt offering on the altar, and place them beside the altar.

**11** [MT 6:4] Then he shall take off his garments and put on other garments, and carry the ashes outside the camp to a clean place.

**12** [MT 6:5] The fire on the altar shall be kept burning on it; it shall not go out. The priest shall burn wood on it every morning, and he shall arrange the burnt offering on it and burn on it the fat of the peace offerings.

**13** [MT 6:6] A perpetual fire shall be kept burning on the altar; it shall not go out.

**14** [MT 6:7] "And this is the instruction of the grain offering: the sons of Aaron shall offer it before the LORD, in front of the altar.

**15** [MT 6:8] And the priest shall take up from it with his handful from the fine flour of the grain offering and from its oil, and all the frankincense that is on the grain offering, and he shall burn it on the altar as a pleasing aroma — its memorial portion — to the LORD.

**16** [MT 6:9] And the remainder of it Aaron and his sons shall eat; it shall be eaten without leaven in the court of the tent of meeting.

**17** [MT 6:10] It shall not be baked with leaven. I have given it as their portion of my fire offerings; it is most holy, like the sin offering and like the guilt offering.

**18** [MT 6:11] Every male among the sons of Aaron shall eat it — a perpetual statute throughout your generations from the fire offerings of the LORD; everything that touches them shall become holy.

**19** [MT 6:12] And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**20** [MT 6:13] "This is the offering of Aaron and his sons that they shall offer to the LORD on the day when he is anointed: a tenth of an ephah of fine flour as a regular grain offering, half of it in the morning and half of it in the evening.

**21** [MT 6:14] On a griddle it shall be made with oil; you shall bring it well mixed — broken pieces of the grain offering — you shall offer it as a pleasing aroma to the LORD.

**22** [MT 6:15] And the anointed priest who is in his place from his sons shall prepare it — a perpetual statute to the LORD; it shall be wholly burned.

**23** [MT 6:16] Every grain offering of a priest shall be wholly burned; it shall not be eaten."

**24** [MT 6:17] And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**25** [MT 6:18] "Speak to Aaron and his sons, saying: This is the instruction of the sin offering. In the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered, the sin offering shall be slaughtered before the LORD — it is most holy.

**26** [MT 6:19] The priest who offers it for sin shall eat it; in the court of the tent of meeting it shall be eaten.

**27** [MT 6:20] Everything that touches its flesh shall become holy; and when any of its blood is splashed on a garment, you shall wash that on which it was splashed in a holy place.

**28** [MT 6:21] And the earthen vessel in which it was boiled shall be broken; and if it was boiled in a bronze vessel, that vessel shall be scoured and rinsed in water.

**29** [MT 6:22] Every male among the priests shall eat it — it is most holy.

**30** [MT 6:23] But no sin offering from which any blood was brought into the tent of meeting to make atonement in the holy place shall be eaten; it shall be burned with fire.

---

## SOPHIA's Arbitration Notes

### Chapter Overview

Leviticus 6 (MT 6:1-23; English 6:8-30) is the opening panel of the priestly officiants' manual. Chapters 1-5 addressed the laity as offerers; chapters 6-7 address Aaron and his sons as officiants. The structural marker is the formula tsav ("Command") in v.9 — a stronger directive verb than the public-address dabber/emor of the earlier chapters — and the repeated heading zot torat ha-... ("This is the instruction of the..."), which introduces each new section of priestly procedure.

The chapter is organized into four sections:

| Section | Formula | MT Verses | English Verses | Subject |
|---------|---------|-----------|----------------|---------|
| I | Zot torat ha-olah | 6:1-6 | 6:8-13 | Burnt offering: overnight burn, ash removal, perpetual fire |
| II | Zot torat ha-minchah | 6:7-11 | 6:14-18 | Grain offering: priest's portion, most-holy status |
| III | (Continuation) | 6:12-16 | 6:19-23 | High priest's daily grain offering: wholly burned |
| IV | Zot torat ha-chattat | 6:17-23 | 6:24-30 | Sin offering: slaughter-place, contagious holiness, vessel rules |

The governing theological concept throughout is qodesh qodashim — "most holy" — appearing at vv.17, 25, and 29. It is the chapter's supreme category, determining who may eat (male priests only), where (the court of the tent of meeting), how (without leaven), and what must be done when holiness escapes its expected boundaries (blood on garments, absorbed holiness in vessels).

---

### Key Arbitration Decisions

**1. torah = "instruction" (vv.9, 14, 25).** All three translators agree: torah here carries its basic sense of procedural directive (from yarah, "to teach, to direct"), not "the Torah" as canonical whole. The formula zot torat ha-olah / ha-minchah / ha-chattat introduces each section as the operational protocol for a specific offering type. SOPHIA adopts "instruction" throughout, consistent with the project-wide convention established in Genesis and carried through Leviticus.

**2. esh tamid = "perpetual fire" (vv.12-13).** EMET uses "perpetual fire shall be kept burning" (v.13); LOGOS uses "fire shall be kept burning continually"; PNEUMA uses "The fire must be kept continually burning." SOPHIA adopts EMET's "A perpetual fire shall be kept burning on the altar; it shall not go out." The phrase esh tamid is a theologically freighted compound — tamid ("perpetual/continual/regular") governs the entire altar-fire theology and connects to the tamid burnt offering and the tamid bread of the Presence. Preserving "perpetual fire" as a compound noun at v.13, where the standalone declaratory statement appears, best serves both accuracy and the LXX's own rendering (pyr to dia pantos). At v.12 the embedded version is rendered as the procedural "The fire on the altar shall be kept burning on it; it shall not go out."

**3. chattat = "sin offering" throughout (vv.17, 25, 26, 29, 30).** PNEUMA uses "purification offering" for chattat in all sin-offering contexts throughout this chapter. EMET and LOGOS use "sin offering." Per the project directive and the ruling established in LEV_004 and LEV_005, this project renders chattat as "sin offering." PNEUMA's "purification offering" reflects a legitimate scholarly position (Milgrom, Gane) regarding the primary function of the chattat as effecting purification of the sanctuary, but the project convention is "sin offering" and SOPHIA upholds it. See disagreement log D-LEV006-15 through D-LEV006-19 for full record.

**4. deshen = "ashes" (v.10).** EMET uses "ashes" and in notes specifies "fatty ash." LOGOS uses "the ashes to which the fire has reduced the burnt offering." PNEUMA uses "the fatty ashes of the burnt offering that the fire has consumed." SOPHIA renders: "the ashes that the fire has consumed of the burnt offering on the altar." The deshen is technically the fatty residue of the burnt offering (from dashan, "to be fat"), but incorporating "fatty" into the running text produces an unusual English phrase. The participial construction "that the fire has consumed" preserves the MT's to'khal ha-esh (literally "that the fire devoured") without over-literalizing the anthropomorphism. LOGOS has the closest model; SOPHIA compresses slightly for flow.

**5. linen vestments (v.10).** EMET has "linen garment" and "linen breeches." LOGOS has "linen garment" and "linen undergarment." PNEUMA has "linen robe" and "linen undergarments." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "linen garment" for mido vad (the outer priestly tunic) and "linen undergarment" for miknese vad (the linen leg covering). "Breeches" (EMET) is archaic; "robe" (PNEUMA) inflates the garment's formality. "Undergarment" accurately indicates the miknese as worn next to the body (al-besaro, "on/against his flesh"), which SOPHIA preserves as "on his body."

**6. ash removal verb (v.11).** EMET has "strip off his garments"; LOGOS and PNEUMA have "take off his garments." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "take off his garments." The Hebrew u-fashat means to strip off or remove, and EMET's "strip off" is not incorrect, but "take off" is the more natural modern English idiom and carries the sense without suggesting violence to the garments.

**7. azkarah = "memorial portion" (v.15).** EMET renders azkarah as "representative portion"; LOGOS and PNEUMA use "memorial portion." SOPHIA rules LOGOS/PNEUMA: "memorial portion." The root z-k-r (to remember, bring to remembrance) is the semantic heart of azkarah — it is the portion that "reminds" or "memorializes" before the LORD, a token that causes the whole to be accepted. "Representative" captures function but loses the remembrance dimension. The LXX's mnemosynon confirms the memorial-remembrance rendering. This ruling is consistent with the decision in LEV_005 (D-LEV005-18).

**8. priestly remainder eating (v.16).** LOGOS has "It shall be eaten without leaven in a holy place. In the court of the tent of meeting they shall eat it." EMET has "unleavened it shall be eaten in the court of the tent of meeting; they shall eat it." PNEUMA has "It is to be eaten without yeast, in the court of the tent of meeting." SOPHIA resolves the double te'akhel structure by following LOGOS's flow: "it shall be eaten without leaven in the court of the tent of meeting." The MT has two separate te'akhel clauses (be-maqom qadosh te'akhel / matsot te'akhel), but SOPHIA consolidates for English readability while retaining both spatial ("court of the tent of meeting") and material ("without leaven") qualifications. "Leaven" is maintained over PNEUMA's "yeast" — "leaven" is the established project term and carries the ritual-purity connotation of the Hebrew chametz more precisely than the culinary "yeast."

**9. chuqat olam = "perpetual statute" (vv.18, 22).** EMET uses "perpetual due" at v.18 and "perpetual statute" at v.22; LOGOS uses "perpetual statute" at both; PNEUMA uses "permanent entitlement" (v.18) and "permanent obligation" (v.22). SOPHIA rules "perpetual statute" consistently (LOGOS). The Hebrew chuqat olam is a standing formulaic phrase meaning "a statute/ordinance forever/in perpetuity" — a permanent institutional right and obligation. "Statute" best preserves its legislative character. "Due" (EMET, v.18) overemphasizes the receiving side; "entitlement" and "obligation" (PNEUMA) each capture only half of the formula's dual force (right and duty together).

**10. kalil = "wholly burned" (vv.22-23).** EMET uses "wholly burned"; LOGOS uses "burned in its entirety"; PNEUMA uses "completely burned." SOPHIA adopts EMET's "wholly burned" as a consistent rendering of kalil — a single-word English equivalent that is both concise and precise, capturing the sense of kalil (complete, entire, whole consumed sacrifice) without the lengthier "in its entirety." The LXX's holokautoma for kalil (at v.23) confirms the whole-burning sense.

**11. "the anointed priest who is in his place from his sons" (v.22).** EMET has the most literal reading: "the anointed priest who is in his place from his sons." LOGOS: "the priest who is anointed in his place from among his sons." PNEUMA: "Whichever of Aaron's sons is the anointed priest who succeeds him." SOPHIA adopts EMET's phrasing as the closest to the MT ha-kohen ha-mashiach asher yimashach tachatav mi-banav, which describes the successor high priest anointed "in his place" (tachatav — in Aaron's place) from among his sons. The dynastic continuity is important: the daily grain offering is a perpetual obligation that passes from high priest to high priest.

**12. nizzah = "splashed" vs "sprinkled" vs "spatters" (v.27).** EMET uses "splashed" (twice, for the active and passive); LOGOS uses "sprinkled"; PNEUMA uses "spatters." SOPHIA rules EMET: "splashed." The Hebrew yizzeh / nizzah (from n-z-h) describes an inadvertent spattering of blood during slaughter or blood manipulation — not a deliberate cultic sprinkling (which uses different vocabulary: hizah, in the hiphil, for the intentional ritual sprinkle). "Splashed" captures the inadvertent, accidental character of the event. "Sprinkled" (LOGOS) risks confusion with the technical ritual sprinkling vocabulary. "Spatters" (PNEUMA) is accurate but colloquial. "Splashed" achieves the right register.

**13. "earthen vessel" vs "clay vessel" (v.28).** EMET and LOGOS use "earthen vessel"; PNEUMA uses "clay vessel." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "earthen vessel." The Hebrew kli cheres ("vessel of pottery/earthenware") is accurately rendered by "earthen vessel," which has the advantage of being the established English liturgical and legal term for pottery vessels in priestly contexts (cf. Lev 11:33; Num 5:17). "Clay vessel" is not wrong, but "earthen" is the more precise liturgical register.

**14. "every male among the priests" (v.29).** EMET and LOGOS have "every male among the priests"; PNEUMA has "any male among the priests." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "every male among the priests." The Hebrew kol-zakhar ba-kohanim uses kol ("every/all"), expressing totality — all male priests may eat, without exception. "Any" (PNEUMA) introduces a more permissive or conditional sense ("any one of them who chooses to"), whereas "every" maintains the totality of the priestly eating right.

**15. v.30 — final clause.** EMET and LOGOS have "it shall be burned with fire"; PNEUMA has "it must be burned in the fire." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "it shall be burned with fire." The Hebrew ba-esh tissaref renders naturally as "shall be burned with fire," maintaining the declarative future-tense form consistent with the legislative style of the chapter. PNEUMA's "must" imports an English modal obligation marker that is already expressed by the declarative form.

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The perpetual fire as theological center.** The esh tamid command (vv.12-13) is the chapter's theological heart. Its double statement — first embedded in the morning wood-stacking procedure (v.12) and then as a standalone absolute declaration (v.13) — creates the chapter's most memorable theological affirmation: the altar fire never sleeps. The priestly style here moves from procedure to principle, from instruction to covenant statement. The fire that descended from the LORD's presence at the tabernacle's inauguration (Lev 9:24) must be maintained by human faithfulness — morning by morning, generation by generation, without exception.

**The ash ritual as priestly dignity.** Verses 10-11 describe the ash removal in two stages: (1) the priest in full linen vestments lifts the ashes and places them beside the altar (a formal presentation); (2) he changes garments and carries the ashes outside to a clean place. Two changes of clothing for what appears to be a maintenance task. The text frames this as a fully priestly act: even the residue of the divine fire carries holiness sufficient to require official vestments. The space outside the camp is still maqom tahor (clean place) — the ash retains its consecrated identity even in disposal.

**The grain offering's two-phase structure.** The grain offering legislation (vv.14-18) operates by dividing the offering into the azkarah (the handful with all frankincense, burned on the altar) and the remainder (eaten by male priests in the court, without leaven). The remainder is not a leftover or afterthought — it is explicitly designated qodesh qodashim (most holy), ranked alongside the sin and guilt offerings. The priests' eating is not a benefit incidental to their role; it is a covenantal participation in the offering, a consumption of holy food in holy space.

**The high priest's daily offering — the prayer he cannot eat.** Verses 19-23 establish the minchah tamid of the anointed high priest: a tenth of an ephah of fine flour daily, half morning and half evening, prepared on a griddle with oil, brought in broken pieces. The structural climax of this subsection is v.23: "Every grain offering of a priest shall be wholly burned; it shall not be eaten." The logic is clean: a person cannot benefit from an offering made on their own behalf. The lay person's grain offering is received by the priest (whose eating is the covenantal mediation); but the priest has no one above him to receive his portion. So the high priest's daily offering is entirely surrendered — kalil, wholly consumed. This makes it structurally equivalent to the olah (burnt offering), as the LXX's choice of holokautoma for kalil quietly notes.

**Contagious holiness in the sin offering (vv.27-28).** The sin offering's blood and flesh carry a holiness so intense that it is transmissible by contact. Blood splashed on a garment requires washing in a holy place — not merely laundering, but processing within the sacred precincts. An earthen vessel that cooked the sin offering flesh cannot be purified (pottery is porous; it absorbs and retains the holiness permanently) and must be broken. A bronze vessel can be scoured and rinsed and restored to ritual neutrality. This is a consistent application of a principle about matter and holiness: some things, once penetrated by the most holy, are permanently altered; others can be cleansed.

**The non-edible sin offering (v.30).** The chapter closes with a principled exception to the rule that the officiating priest eats the sin offering: when the blood of a sin offering is brought inside the tent of meeting to make atonement in the holy place — the inner-sanctuary atonement procedure for the anointed priest or the whole congregation (Lev 4:1-21) — the flesh cannot be eaten and must be wholly burned. The same circularity principle applies as with the high priest's grain offering: when the officiant is also the beneficiary of the atonement, the offering cannot return to him. Both exceptions in chapter 6 — the kalil grain offering and the inside-blood sin offering — share this logic: when there is no mediator above you, your offering is entirely surrendered.

---

### Term Renderings Established or Confirmed (Leviticus 6)

| Hebrew | SOPHIA Rendering | Notes |
|--------|-----------------|-------|
| תּוֹרָה (torah, cultic) | instruction | Procedural directive for a specific offering type |
| אֵשׁ תָּמִיד (esh tamid) | perpetual fire | LXX: pyr to dia pantos |
| מוֹקְדָה (moqedah) | hearth | The burning-place of the altar; hapax |
| דֶּשֶׁן (deshen) | ashes | Fatty residue of the burnt offering |
| אַזְכָּרָה (azkarah) | memorial portion | z-k-r root; LXX: mnemosynon |
| קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים (qodesh qodashim) | most holy | Supreme sacral category; LXX: hagia ton hagion |
| מַצּוֹת (matsot) | without leaven | Leaven (chametz) excluded from priestly portions |
| כָּלִיל (kalil) | wholly burned | Complete consumption on altar; LXX: holokautoma |
| תָּמִיד (tamid) | perpetual / regular / continual | Context-dependent; consistent with Num tamid usage |
| מֹרַק (moraq) | scoured | Intensive cleaning of bronze vessel; hapax in Lev |
| בַּקֹּדֶשׁ (ba-qodesh) | in the holy place | The inner sanctuary where atonement blood is applied |
| חַטָּאת (chattat) | sin offering | Established project convention (upheld from LEV_004) |
| עֹלָה (olah) | burnt offering | Established from LEV_001 |
| מִנְחָה (minchah) | grain offering | Established from LEV_002 |
| שְׁלָמִים (shelamim) | peace offerings | Established from LEV_003 |
| אָשָׁם (asham) | guilt offering | Established from LEV_005 |
| אִשֶּׁה (isheh) | fire offering | Established from LEV_001 |
| רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ (re'ach nichoach) | pleasing aroma | Established from LEV_001 |
| יהוה | the LORD | Project convention throughout |
| אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד | tent of meeting | Established convention |

— SOPHIA
